_description: Single source of truth for all numbers used in the paper. Every value here is verified and timestamped. Use ONLY these numbers when writing the paper.
_paper: Causal Direction Decomposition (CDD): Three Mechanistic Regimes of Truthfulness in Large Language Models

H1_probe_circuit_misalignment._claim: Linear probes and causal circuits measure near-orthogonal structures despite both correlating with truthfulness labels

circuit._claim: A 50-component circuit achieves 35.9x specificity with 546% ablation drop

CRR._claim: 100% of circuit components recover rank-1 causal direction (CRR >= 0.70)

three_regime_structure._claim: 22 TruthfulQA domains separate into three statistically distinct regimes

Direction-Driven.description: Global causal direction active; CPA high

Circuit-Failure.description: Contested knowledge; no stable causal direction

ablation_A_K_sensitivity._claim: CPA is stable across circuit sizes K=10 to K=100 (range=0.0160)
ablation_A_K_sensitivity.paper_sentence: CPA remains stable across K (range=0.016, all values < 0.10). Specificity peaks at K=75 (1831x), confirming a hard circuit boundary at K=50.

ablation_B_stage2_bypass._claim: cPCA direction source is necessary; mean-diff direction is surface-dominated

cpca_full_method.note: Full CDD method — deep causal structure

ablation_B_stage2_bypass.paper_sentence: cPCA CPA (0.0666) << mean-diff CPA (0.4138), confirming Stage 2 isolates deep causal structure rather than surface label shifts.

ablation_C_probe_regularisation._claim: CPA is stable across six orders of magnitude of probe regularisation
ablation_C_probe_regularisation.paper_sentence: CPA remains below 0.10 across six orders of magnitude of probe regularisation (C=0.01 to 1000, range=0.0054).

replication._claim: H1 confirmed across two architectures, two training corpora, two datasets

Pythia_1.4B_TruthfulQA.note: Cross-architecture replication — different training corpus (The Pile)

null_expectation.interpretation: Expected CPA if probe direction were uniformly random in R^d
